stories.title: Live updates: US-Iran strikes; conflict widens with Houthis claiming Red Sea attacks
stories.summary: US President Donald Trump has threatened to destroy one bridge or power plant for every ship Iran hits in the Strait of Hormuz, amid escalating tensions between the two countries. Yemen's Iran-backed Houthi rebels have claimed to have struck two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea, widening the conflict. The US military has conducted its 12th night of strikes against Iran, aimed at degrading its ability to threaten civilian mariners and commercial vessels. The standoff in the Gulf shows no signs of progress in diplomatic efforts to end the conflict.

stories.title: Pit found by Quebecer confirmed to be a 390-million-years-old meteorite impact crater
stories.summary: A 390-million-year-old meteorite impact crater has been confirmed in Quebec, Canada. The crater, approximately 15 miles wide, was discovered by amateur astronomer Joël Lapointe on Google Maps in 2024. Researchers, led by planetary geologist Gordon Osinski, have verified the crater's existence, with findings set to be presented at the Annual Meeting of the Meteoritical Society in Germany. This significant discovery sheds new light on the region's geological history.

stories.title: JPO Rejects Nintendo Patent in Palworld Case
stories.summary: The Japan Patent Office has rejected Nintendo's patent application 2024-031879 for lack of inventive step, citing prior art from games including ARK, Monster Hunter 4, and Pokémon GO. The patent targeted an "in-game monster-capturing system" similar to Palworld's gameplay mechanics. This rejection does not directly impact Nintendo's ongoing lawsuit against Palworld, but it does undermine the company's patent claims. The decision highlights the existence of similar gameplay mechanics in older games, including a 2013 YouTube video of an unofficial Pokémon game.

stories.title: Before ‘Jana Nayagan’: When an 18-year-old Vijay walked the halls of Loyola College
stories.summary: Actor Vijay, now Tamil Nadu's Chief Minister, is releasing his last film as a solo hero, Jana Nayagan, on July 23, 2026. The movie marks a return to his acting career after 34 years, following his debut in 1992 with Naalaiya Theerpu at the age of 18 while studying at Loyola College in Chennai. Jana Nayagan is a celebration of Vijay's stardom and his evolution as a political personality. Fans are eagerly awaiting the film's release, with a unique promotional campaign featuring a flying LED display creating buzz on social media platforms.

stories.title: Scottish Labour leader Anas Sarwar 'delighted' to accept trade minister job
stories.summary: Scottish Labour leader Anas Sarwar is stepping down to become Minister of State for Trade in the UK government, a role he will take up alongside a peerage in the House of Lords. He will leave the Scottish Parliament and his leader role, having led the party since 2021. Sarwar's appointment comes after Scottish Labour won just 17 seats in the May Scottish Parliament elections, a record low for the party. He will work with his successor to serve people in Scotland.

stories.title: Charity 'heartbroken' as food ruined in break-in
stories.summary: A charity warehouse in Middlesbrough was broken into on Tuesday, resulting in 16 tonnes of food being ruined, equivalent to 7,000 meals. Thieves smashed their way in, cutting power to the freezer and chiller units, forcing the charity to discard perishable items. FareShare North East, which distributes food to over 200 community groups, is "heartbroken" over the loss. The charity is now assessing the damage and working to recover from the break-in.

stories.title: Google burning through cash with spiralling AI costs
stories.summary: Google's parent company Alphabet reported negative free cash flow of $5.9 billion for the first time in at least a decade, due to soaring spending on artificial intelligence infrastructure. The company's AI costs are expected to reach $205 billion this year, up from $190 billion. This surge in spending comes as Alphabet's combined quarterly revenue hit $119.8 billion, a 23% increase from last year. The company's stock fell 4% in after-hours trading following the announcement.

stories.title: HNK Rijeka tie 'a great opportunity' for Derry
stories.summary: Derry City Football Club has been fined 20,000 euros and given a suspended partial stadium closure for two years after crowd trouble marred their Europa League qualifier against CSKA Sofia. The match was temporarily stopped and police deployed due to rival supporter disturbances. Derry's manager, Lynch, sees the tie as a great opportunity to build ahead of their opening Conference League qualifying game. The team will now focus on regrouping and preparing for their next match.

stories.title: Airport drop-off fees up by a third - here are the priciest
stories.summary: Airport drop-off fees have increased by a third on average since last summer, with London's Gatwick airport charging a £10 "farewell fee" for 10 minutes. The RAC analysis found 16 of the 20 busiest UK airports have raised their prices, with London City Airport now the most expensive at £8 for five minutes. Birmingham remains the cheapest at £7 for 15 minutes. Passengers can expect to pay more for drop-offs at UK airports as the fees are now a standard part of their business models.

stories.title: Business rates to be cut by 20% for pubs, clubs and music venues in England
stories.summary: Business rates for nearly 32,000 pubs, clubs, and music venues in England will be cut by 20% from April, saving the typical pub £1,100 next year. The £100m package is fully funded, with some business reliefs being reviewed. This move is part of the new administration's efforts to ease living costs. The impact of other cost of living measures, including capping bus fares at £2, remains to be seen.

stories.title: River levels approach all-time low as drought announcement looms
stories.summary: River levels in Wales are approaching all-time lows, with the country experiencing just 5% of its average July rainfall due to back-to-back heatwaves and a dry spring. This has led to significant problems, including fish and wildlife deaths, failing crops, and heat-stressed livestock. An announcement from Natural Resources Wales is expected later on Thursday, as the region moves towards an environmental drought. The prolonged dry weather is affecting north and south-east Wales.

stories.title: 'I am still alive': Indian activist on hunger strike for 26 days loses 11kg
stories.summary: Indian activist Tenzin Wangchuk, on a 26-day hunger strike, has lost 11kg and was admitted to the ICU at Medanta Hospital in Gurugram. He remains stable, but refuses to call off his strike despite requests from opposition MPs, activists, and celebrities. Health Minister JP Nadda has also urged him to break his fast and join the mainstream. Wangchuk's health concerns have mounted as he endured 21 days in scorching summer heat on a diet of salt and water.

stories.title: Ex-UFC champion Andrade working as delivery driver
stories.summary: Former UFC strawweight champion Jessica Andrade is working as a delivery driver to earn extra money while recovering from injury. The 34-year-old Brazilian has not competed since August, when she injured a shoulder. Andrade has fought 30 times in the UFC, winning 17 of her fights. She plans to use her new skills to supplement her income if needed.

stories.title: Weekly sports quiz: Glasgow 2026, The Open and The Hundred
stories.summary: A record number of teams, approximately 6%, achieved full marks in last week's sports quiz. The quiz focused on recent events including the Commonwealth Games build-up in Glasgow and the start of The Hundred. This week's quiz is now available, with notifications sent to devices for those who signed up.

stories.title: Russia's businesses under strain from Ukraine's attacks on Wildberries
stories.summary: Ukrainian drones have hit two warehouses belonging to Russia's biggest online retailer, Wildberries, in the past weekend, killing seven workers and injuring dozens in the Tambov region. The attacks, which also damaged goods worth an estimated £750m, are part of Kyiv's strategy to disrupt supplies to the Russian army. Hundreds of Russian sellers who depend on online marketplaces for their livelihoods are now facing financial losses. The war is taking a toll on small and medium-sized businesses in Russia.

stories.title: Butterfly city blooms in shadow of office blocks
stories.summary: In Hull city centre, a "butterfly city" has been created with ten species of butterflies spotted in a mini meadow near St Stephen's Shopping Centre. The site, owned by the Land Registry, features grass and wild flowers that have been allowed to grow, providing a habitat for the butterflies. Small skipper butterflies have been breeding in the meadow, a rare occurrence in a city. The meadow was established in 2023 as part of a scheme to encourage city centre wildlife.

stories.title: Ann Widdecombe public memorial to be held later this year, family says
stories.summary: A public memorial for Ann Widdecombe will be held later this year, following her private funeral for family and close friends in the coming weeks. The 78-year-old former Conservative minister and Reform UK spokeswoman was found dead with serious head injuries earlier this month. Her family thanked the public for condolences but asked to limit the initial service to those closest to her. The memorial details will be released in due course.

stories.title: Blocked by censors, China's animal lovers take fight against abuse offline and overseas
stories.summary: Thousands of Chinese animal lovers are taking to social media and billboards in New York to protest the horrific killing of a stray dog and her puppies, despite authorities urging them to withhold comments and scrubbing online posts. The incident sparked outrage in June, with authorities citing the age of four boys involved as a reason for the censorship. The restrictions have only fueled the outcry, with posts about the incident still drawing thousands of comments three weeks later. The movement is now going global, with supporters abroad joining the fight against animal abuse.

stories.title: ServiceNow bets $40 million on Indian banking software specialist to expand its financial services push
stories.summary: ServiceNow has invested $40 million in Indian banking software specialist BusinessNext, valuing the company at $700 million and taking a 5% stake. This deal expands their partnership in AI for financial services and gives BusinessNext access to ServiceNow's global sales network. BusinessNext serves over 70 banks across India, Southeast Asia, the Middle East, and the US, with half of its revenue coming from outside India. The partnership is expected to drive growth for BusinessNext in overseas markets.

stories.title: Global Oil Prices Rise as Conflict With Iran Deepens
stories.summary: The Houthis claimed to have attacked two ships in the Red Sea, and Iran said a third caught fire near the Strait of Hormuz.

stories.title: U.S. Military Presence in Jordan Makes It Iranian Target
stories.summary: Relying heavily on U.S. aid and protection, Jordan is unlikely to restrict the American military presence there, despite escalating Iranian airstrikes.

stories.title: How the Trump Administration’s Nuclear Deal With Saudi Arabia May Work
stories.summary: The Trump administration announced an agreement with Saudi Arabia on a civilian nuclear program. Such deals, known as 123 Agreements, are designed to prevent the development of weapons.

stories.title: Alarm over hospital lung admissions for children in England’s poorest postcodes
stories.summary: In England's poorest postcodes, children are 40% more likely to be admitted to hospital for respiratory infections. According to NHS England data, 1,941 out of every 100,000 children in the most deprived areas were hospitalized for respiratory illnesses in 2024/25, a 41% higher rate than in the least deprived areas. Experts are raising concerns about the impact of air pollution on young people's health. Further analysis is needed to determine the exact cause of these hospital admissions.

stories.title: Olympic Mountain Glory
stories.summary: The Olympic Mountains in northwestern Washington state stand at over 7,000 feet, their rugged terrain carved by deep river valleys. Located within Olympic National Park, the mountains' imposing presence is a result of layers of basalt from undersea eruptions that formed between 55 and 15 million years ago. The park's diverse landscape also includes temperate rainforests, wild coastlines, and blue waters. As the region remains a relatively remote bastion of wilderness, conservation efforts continue to protect its unique ecosystem.

stories.title: Fatboy Slim helps to save 'lifeblood' music venue
stories.summary: Fatboy Slim's Maverick Ventures Partners has purchased its tenth music venue, making it the largest landlord of dedicated music venues in the country. This move comes as more than half of the UK's grassroots venues made no profit last year, with 30 closing and 6,000 jobs lost. The purchase secures the future of The Pipeline in Brighton, a key space for musicians to develop their craft. The venue's owner, Thomas Evrenos, is relieved, saying it will allow him to continue running the venue without fear of increased rent.

stories.title: Courtney Stodden Is Fighting for Something Bigger Than Jason Alexander’s Apology
stories.summary: Jason Alexander has issued a public apology to Courtney Stodden, 14.5 years after a 2012 comedy sketch on Funny or Die's YouTube channel featured him fondling Stodden's breasts with a cell phone when she was 17. The sketch, a parody of "Dr. Phil," has since been taken down. Alexander's apology comes as Stodden continues to advocate for greater accountability in the entertainment industry.

stories.title: ‘The Wild Robot’ VFX Studio STIM Sets Canary Islands Operation, Planning to Create More Than 100 Jobs
stories.summary: French animation and visual effects company STIM is opening a production center in the Canary Islands, Spain, with plans to create over 100 jobs. The operation, STIM Tenerife, is set to begin in August and aims to employ 60 people by the end of 2026. Located in the Canary Islands Special Zone, a low-tax regime, the new base will increase STIM's production capacity without compromising artistic and technical excellence. Recruitment for high-skilled positions is already underway.

stories.title: Lego’s Donkey Kong arcade machine lets Mario jump endless barrels — Miyamoto is reportedly happy
stories.summary: A Lego Donkey Kong arcade machine has been created, priced at $200, featuring a working joystick and button to control Mario's movement and jumping. The game includes an endless series of barrels for Mario to jump, utilizing the same original barrels first released in 1989 for Lego's pirate sets. The machine does not play like the original Donkey Kong, but it contains a basic fully functional game. Shigeru Miyamoto, creator of Mario, is reportedly pleased with the design.

stories.title: Life-changing MS drug to help people walk now on NHS in England
stories.summary: A life-changing multiple sclerosis treatment, fampridine, is now available on the NHS in England, benefiting up to 5,000 people annually. This "signal booster" drug helps nerve signals, allowing some individuals to walk independently. Clinical trials showed nearly 40% of users experienced improvements in walking speed and duration. The decision is expected to make a significant difference in the lives of those with multiple sclerosis.

stories.title: Clarity Act Mired In Debate Over Whether to Bar President From Selling Crypto
stories.summary: Democrats and Republicans are haggling about the Clarity Act, a major bill pushed by the crypto industry, as it moves closer to a Senate vote.

offbeat.title: Live updates: US-Iran strikes; conflict widens with Houthis claiming Red Sea attacks
offbeat.summary: US President Donald Trump has threatened to destroy one bridge or power plant for every ship Iran hits in the Strait of Hormuz, amid escalating tensions between the two countries. Yemen's Iran-backed Houthi rebels have claimed to have struck two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea, widening the conflict.
